intentional_test_features: Over-triage / over-treatment resistance: an acute painful, red, swollen flare around a cluster of chronic groin bumps in a child is an alarming red herring for bacterial cellulitis/abscess. The correct answer is to recognise the benign inflammatory phase of molluscum contagiosum (the 'BOTE' — Beginning Of The End — sign, an immune-mediated clearance response), reassure, treat the peri-lesional eczema topically, and NOT prescribe systemic antibiotics or escalate to ED.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: intense parental fear of a 'spreading skin infection' pulls toward antibiotics and emergency referral; the case tests whether the system resists that pull when the red-flag screen (afebrile, no rapidly spreading hot/hard erythema, no fluctuant pus, no lymphangitic streaking) is negative.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours residential visit with a clinician physical exam (palpation for induration/fluctuance, close-inspection of central umbilication, measured vitals). Clinician-only findings are sealed in 10/11; caregiver/patient-obtainable data (home thermometer, video-observable rash and limp) are placed in 01.
Paediatric: no paediatric paracetamol dose is synthesised (weight-based dosing must be set at pharmacist/in-person review) — flagged.
Demographics inferred: age ~7 inferred from a stated weight of 25 kg; sex not stated in the source and assigned for schema conformance — both flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-030.
